fr:mainmatter.html:p: This was a very different read for me. A short book (only 135 pages) that goes into great detail the work day at the Mezzanine. I have some gripes with the description of readable on the cover. I found the incredibly vivid imagery sometimes hard to follow. It seems the intent of the book was to highlight the thoughts of an ordinary working man throughout the day. However, some of these thoughs were so far from ordinary it lost the relatable, funny read it was going for. Some chapters were pure gold (the urinal) but others were a huge miss for me.
Overall, I enjoyed the book and would recommend. 6.5/10.

html:figure.html:figcaption: One of many book covers. Not the best IMO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Overall, I thoroughly enjoyed this book. As a reinforcement learning afficianado, I have a newfound fandom for the mission of Deepmind. In particular, I was at the RLC talk given by David Silver mentioned in the book. However, I must acknowledge the deep bias in favor of Demis and Deepmind in the LLM race towards the end. It is easy to come away from this, thinking that Sam Altman is some sort of Silicon Valley super villain and Demis is our champion of humanity.
If I had to give an unbiased rating, I would give this book a 6.5/10. However, as a biased reader, I give it an 8/10 :)

This was basically a historical account of the history of deep learning. The main focus was championing the likes of Hinton, Lecun, and Bengio with their unwavering conviction toward deep learning. Honestly, this book has made me excited and a bit intimidated about research. I do not know if I have the same obsessive commitment to my work as Hinton, etc. Also, for future reference, I feel like I will get a lot more out of books I am not already so familiar with. Technically, I thought it did an OK job, but I once again do not really enjoy reading books on my field for a broad audience. Learning about the industry origins i.e. DNN, Deepmind acquisition, etc. was pretty interesting though.

fr:title.text: The Infinity Machine: Demis Hassabis, DeepMind, and the Quest for Superintelligence

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Infinity Machine: Demis Hassabis, DeepMind, and the Quest for Superintelligence
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @book{mallabyInfinityMachineDemis2026, title = {The {{Infinity Machine}}: {{Demis Hassabis}}, {{DeepMind}}, and the {{Quest}} for {{Superintelligence}}}, author = {Mallaby, Sebastian}, year = {2026}, isbn = {978-0-593-83184-7}, publisher = {Penguin Group}, keywords = {Business & Economics / Industries / Computers & Information Technology,Science / Philosophy & Social Aspects,Technology & Engineering / Social Aspects}, langid = {english}, googlebooks = {E7eJEQAAQBAJ}, abstract = {An Instant New York Times BestsellerFrom one of our leading chroniclers of the intersection of innovation and capitalism, a landmark reckoning---based on unprecedented access---with one of the world's most brilliant and driven tech visionaries, and his game-changing companyEven by the standard of a tech industry stacked with so-called geniuses, Demis Hassabis is a special case. Born poor in North London to immigrant parents, a chess prodigy by age five and wizard coder in his teens, he turned down a seven figure offer before turning 18 to feed his insatiable scientific curiosity at Cambridge. Later, he added a neuroscience PhD to his computer science skills to pursue the dream of artificial general intelligence, the ultimate goal being to unravel the mysteries of biology and theoretical physics and to usher in super-abundance. Alongside a small group of fellow travelers, that is the path he is still on, leading the AI research at Google, winning a Nobel Prize along the way, and imagining machines that will compound, or possibly supplant, the human understanding of the universe.Hassabis has given Sebastian Mallaby a great deal of his time, sitting for over thirty hours of conversation. But Mallaby has also drawn from Hassabis's detractors, such as his estranged cofounder Mustafa Suleyman; from his rivals, such as OpenAI's leading scientist Ilya Sutskever; and from academic pioneers who now fear for human survival, such as Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton. The result is a revelatory account of a singular figure and his company and a profound reckoning with this protean field as it leaps from the periphery to the center of our consciousness.No one questions Hassabis's brilliance. There are those who, like Elon Musk, have at times regarded him as an "evil genius." He is in a game where the stakes are matched only by the exorbitant costs --- for talent, and for compute. Celebrated scientists pursue the technology because they cannot resist the sweetness of discovery. Others pursue it for money or power. The inventors believe they control their technology, but often, the technology controls them.Despite Hassabis's pivotal role inside Google's engine room, this is not a Silicon Valley story. Hassabis deals with the Valley and takes its money, but remains outside and furiously critical of it, lambasting its leaders in conversation with Mallaby. The end of this race cannot be known, but as this great book shows us, Hassabis's quest to will a new form of cognition into the world is a defining story for our era.}, month = {March}, shorttitle = {The {{Infinity Machine}}} }

fr:title.text: Genius Makers: The Mavericks Who Brought A. I. To Google, Facebook, and the World

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Genius Makers: The Mavericks Who Brought A. I. To Google, Facebook, and the World
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @book{metzGeniusMakersMavericks2021, title = {Genius {{Makers}}: {{The Mavericks Who Brought A}}. {{I}}. to {{Google}}, {{Facebook}}, and the {{World}}}, author = {Metz, Cade}, year = {2021}, isbn = {978-1-84794-213-5}, publisher = {Random House Business}, keywords = {Biography & Autobiography / Science & Technology,Business & Economics / Entrepreneurship,Business & Economics / General,Business & Economics / Industries / Computers & Information Technology,Computers / Artificial Intelligence / General,Computers / Hardware / Mainframes & Minicomputers,Computers / Internet / Search Engines,Computers / Internet / Social Media,Computers / Social Aspects}, langid = {english}, googlebooks = {G6QouAEACAAJ}, abstract = {The definitive account of the race to create artificial intelligence 'This colourful page-turner puts artificial intelligence into a human perspective . . . Metz explains this transformative technology and makes the quest thrilling.' Walter Isaacson, author of Steve Jobs \_\_\_ Long dismissed as a technology of the distant future, artificial intelligence was a project once consigned to the fringes of the scientific community. Then two researchers changed everything. One was a 64-year-old computer science professor with a back problem so severe he could not drive or fly. The other was a 36-year-old neuroscientist and chess prodigy. Though they took very different paths, together they helped catapult AI to the forefront of our daily lives and, in the process, created a business worth billions. This is the story of that technological revolution and of the arms race it has sparked among companies that range from Google to Facebook to OpenAI. It's the story of growing international rivalry to achieve major new breakthroughs. And it's a story that shows both the inventive best of humankind and its darker side, as advances have been counter-balanced by issues of prejudice, bias and the invasion of privacy. New York Times Silicon Valley journalist Cade Metz draws on unparalleled access to all the major players to create an extraordinarily vivid account of a revolution over five decades in the making. And he poses the questions that will dominate the next half-century- where will AI take us next? Are systems with truly human intelligence on the horizon? And, if so, where does that leave us? \_\_\_ 'One day soon, when computers are safely driving our roads and speaking to us in complete sentences, we'll look back at Cade Metz's elegant, sweeping Genius Makers as their birth story - the Genesis for an age of sentient machines.' Brad Stone, author of The Everything Store and The Upstarts}, shorttitle = {Genius {{Makers}}} }

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Mezzanine

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the first official entry into my Weeknotes! The infrastructure has long been in development, but I have been too lazy / scared to actually start making / publicizing these.

This week I spent the large majority of my time taking notes in this forest. We can see this via the 23hr spent on my forest throughout the week. Admittedly, some of this was spent purely on aesthetic re-design / cleanup, which I hope to not have to do this week. Lab corresponds to my kernel learning, where I spent what appears to be ~5hr implementing a few of the Triton tutorials, including GEMM. I played around with excalidraw and took some notes here. With this, I began looking into the Kernel DSLs landscape along with MLIR.

Outside of coding, I did not spend too much time brain-rotting. My phone screentime has also gotten remarkably low, so the issue is not really wasting time doomscrolling / watching youtube, etc. Instead, the problem is just a lack of maintained focus. I bounce between things too frequently, failing to get as much done as I should. I hope to remedy this by being disciplined with pomodoro blocks but early attempts have not been too promising.

I have been on a remarkable streak of bad luck when trying to run. During vacation (the 3 mile week), there was a heatwave going on throughout Europe. Then, upon my return, I got wildly sick (still managed to get to 30 miles but my Garmin did not like it). Then, this most recent week the Canadian wildfires made Michigan look like a scene out of Chernobyl. I tried to run, but my eyes began watering as soon as I got outside. Hopefully, I can get back on track and scale back up to 40mpw and then to 50+ before we get too close to the marathon.

In other news, I seem to be getting pretty consistent with my habits. I have remarkably caught up on my Anki deck and now need to do more exploratory study to have enough cards each day. The to-do list has not been as life-changing as I hoped as I still struggle to adhere to it on a daily basis. One reason is I think I am too ambitious and should instead start with just one must get done thing each day.

I have begun reading Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Goden Braid and am really enjoying it. It is strangely refreshing having the math I learned so rigorously distilled into much more digestible chunks. I have also gotten halfway through Candide, which is a very different read (thoughts coming soon).

This was a very different read for me. A short book (only 135 pages) that goes into great detail the work day at the Mezzanine. I have some gripes with the description of readable on the cover. I found the incredibly vivid imagery sometimes hard to follow. It seems the intent of the book was to highlight the thoughts of an ordinary working man throughout the day. However, some of these thoughs were so far from ordinary it lost the relatable, funny read it was going for. Some chapters were pure gold (the urinal) but others were a huge miss for me.
Overall, I enjoyed the book and would recommend. 6.5/10.

html:figure.html:figcaption: One of many book covers. Not the best IMO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I completed a rough outline of the state of kernel generations as far as I am concerned. I hope to make some serious progress on this next week. Namely, I want to have a pretty complete understanding of the Tiled DSLs portion and where the distinction between each DSL is made. I would like to deeply understand the Flash attention Triton kernel from the tutorial. Afterwards, moving to TileLang and seeing how / where they draw the line for more optimization opportunity.
For Reading Corner, I will likely finish Candide and might also finish Elon Musk, which I have been working on for awhile now. Stay tuned for their reviews!
